severanceTool.source: Worker Retirement Benefits Act §8 (severance system). Average wage calculation can vary by company and industry. Some companies pay severance for under-1-year service.

law.title: Eligibility
law.body: 1+ year continuous service AND average 15+ hours per week (4-week avg). Triggered regardless of voluntary or involuntary termination. Applies to workplaces under 5 too (since 2010-12-01).

average.title: Average wage
average.body: Last 3 months' total wage ÷ days in that period (typically 90). Regular bonus = 1/4 of annual; unused-leave pay = 1/4 of last year's, both added in.

dc.title: DC vs DB
dc.body: DC (defined contribution): 1/12 monthly wage accumulated each year. This calculator assumes DB (defined benefit). DC final amount depends on investment returns.

validation.salaryPositive: Please enter the last 3 months' wage.
validation.hireBeforeResign: Hire date must be before resign date.

weeklyRestPayTool.source: Labor Standards Act §55, Enforcement Decree §30. Company rules and collective agreements may differ.

law.title: LSA §55
law.body: Workers attending all scheduled work days in a week receive 1 paid rest day. 'Full attendance' = no absences. Holidays don't count as absences.

formula.title: Part-time proration
formula.body: Part-time rest pay = (weekly hours ÷ 40) × 8 hours × hourly wage. Capped at 8 hours. 30h/week = 6h pay, 20h/week = 4h pay.

exception.title: Not eligible
exception.body: Under 15 hours/week, weeks without full attendance, daily-hire workers (in principle). Applies to workplaces with under 5 employees too. Employer-caused absences may count as attendance.

validation.wageRequired: Please enter hourly or monthly wage.
validation.weeklyHoursPositive: Weekly hours must be greater than 0.

result.heading: Result
result.empty: Enter hourly wage and weekly hours, then convert.

minWageTool.source: Labor Standards Act §55 (weekly rest day). 209 hours is the administrative standard for monthly working hours. Actual pay may differ per company rules.

formula.title: Where does 209 hours come from?
formula.body: 40 weekly work hours + 8 weekly-rest hours = 48 weekly. 52 weeks ÷ 12 ≈ 4.345 weeks/month. 48 × 4.345 ≈ 208.56 → administratively rounded to 209.

partTime.title: Part-time proration
partTime.body: Under 15 weekly hours → no weekly rest pay. 15-40 hours → weekly rest pay = (weekly hours / 5) hours per week, capped at 8.

minWage.title: Minimum wage decision
minWage.body: Announced yearly by Aug 5 for the following year. 2025 = 10,030 KRW. 2026 TBD — defaults to 2025 value until announced.

result.empty: Enter an amount and click Calculate.

errors.unknown: An error occurred during calculation.

vatTool.source: Source: VAT Act §30 (10% rate), §63 (simplified taxpayer rates). For reference only — actual filing requires deductible input verification and exempt-sales segregation.

law.title: VAT Act §30
law.body: 10% on supply of goods and services. Supply value = total ÷ 1.1. Businesses file by netting sales tax against input tax.

simple.title: Simplified taxpayer
simple.body: Prior-year sales under KRW 104M. Industry value-added rate (15-40%) is applied, then 10% VAT. No mandatory tax invoice issuance.

deadline.title: Filing deadlines
deadline.body: General: H1 (Jan-Jun) by Jul 25, H2 (Jul-Dec) by Jan 25 next year. Simplified: once a year by Jan 25. Provisional filings due quarterly on the 25th.

annualLeaveTool.source: Source: Labor Standards Act §60 (Annual Paid Leave). Daily ordinary wage = monthly ÷ 209 × 8 per administrative interpretation. For reference only — collective agreements and work rules at your workplace take precedence.

law.title: LSA §60
law.body: 15 days of paid leave per year for ≥ 80% attendance. Sub-1-year or < 80% gets 1 day per fully-attended month. From year 3, +1 day every 2 years; cap 25 days.

compensation.title: Unused-leave pay
compensation.body: Unused days are paid as daily ordinary wage × unused count. Daily wage = monthly ÷ 209 × 8 (base + fixed allowances).

exception.title: Workplaces under 5
exception.body: Workplaces with fewer than 5 regular employees are exempt from annual leave provisions (LSA §11). Collective agreements may override.

validation.hireAfterReference: Hire date cannot be later than reference date.
validation.unusedDaysNegative: Unused days must be 0 or greater.

income-tax.overview: The Korean income tax calculator applies the 8-bracket progressive rate (6% / 15% / 24% / 35% / 38% / 40% / 42% / 45%) as of 2026 to compute calculated tax, final tax, and local income tax from a given taxable base. It supports the wage earner credit (Income Tax Act §59) and shows the effective rate alongside the current bracket. All inputs stay in the browser — nothing is sent to a server.
useCases: Freelancers and sole proprietors: simulate the May income tax filing
Employees: estimate refund or additional payment after year-end settlement
Landlords: see when adding rental income pushes you into a higher bracket
Investors: compare separate vs comprehensive taxation on dividends and interest
Year-end planning: estimate tax reduction from pension or IRP contributions

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your taxable base — gross income minus deductions (personal, pension, special). Employees can read this directly from the year-end settlement document.
If you are an employee, check 'Apply wage earner credit'. The calculator applies the Income Tax Act §59 credit (up to KRW 740,000) automatically.
Click Calculate. You will see the marginal rate, progressive deduction, calculated tax, wage earner credit, final tax, 10% local income tax, and the effective rate.
The 8-bracket table highlights your current bracket. Adjust the taxable base to see how close you are to the next bracket boundary.

formula.title: Formula (Korean Income Tax Act §55)
formula.body:
Calculated tax = Taxable base × Marginal rate − Progressive deduction

2026 brackets:
  up to KRW 14M       : 6%   (deduction 0)
  up to KRW 50M       : 15%  (deduction 1,260,000)
  up to KRW 88M       : 24%  (deduction 5,760,000)
  up to KRW 150M      : 35%  (deduction 15,440,000)
  up to KRW 300M      : 38%  (deduction 19,940,000)
  up to KRW 500M      : 40%  (deduction 25,940,000)
  up to KRW 1B        : 42%  (deduction 35,940,000)
  over KRW 1B         : 45%  (deduction 65,940,000)

Final tax = Calculated tax − tax credits (wage earner, child, etc.)
Local income tax = Final tax × 10%
Total tax = Final + Local

items.title: Example 1: Employee, gross KRW 50M (taxable ~KRW 30M)
items.body: Bracket: 15%. Calculated = 30M × 0.15 − 1.26M = KRW 3.24M. After wage earner credit (~KRW 1.78M) and local tax (~KRW 146K), total roughly KRW 1.6M.

items.title: Example 2: Freelancer, taxable KRW 80M
items.body: Bracket: 24%. Calculated = 80M × 0.24 − 5.76M = KRW 13.44M. No wage earner credit. Plus local 10% = KRW 14.78M total.

items.title: Example 3: Crossing into 35% bracket at KRW 90M
items.body: At KRW 88M you are still at 24%. Adding KRW 2M of income jumps you to 35%: 90M × 0.35 − 15.44M = KRW 16.06M, a jump of about KRW 2.5M for the extra KRW 2M earned. Plan year-end deductions to avoid crossing.

faq.question: When is the Korean income tax filing period?
faq.answer: May 1 to May 31 each year. Sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers get an extension to June 30. Payment is due in the same window, with optional installment to 2 months later.

faq.question: Do employees need to file?
faq.answer: If you only have wage income, the year-end settlement handles it. If you have side income, freelance income, rental income, or financial income above KRW 20M, you must file in May.

faq.question: What is the progressive deduction?
faq.answer: A shortcut for the staircase tax formula. Instead of computing tax bracket by bracket, you multiply the entire base by the top marginal rate and subtract the progressive deduction for that bracket — the result is identical.

faq.question: Where do I file local income tax?
faq.answer: Local income tax is 10% of the final tax and is filed alongside income tax via Hometax. A separate payment notice is issued and paid to your city or district office.

faq.question: What is the wage earner credit cap?
faq.answer: Up to KRW 740K for gross income below KRW 33M; the cap decreases for higher incomes (KRW 660K / 500K / 200K floor at high incomes). 55% credit on calculated tax up to KRW 1.3M, then 30%, capped.

faq.question: Does this calculator handle child or pension credits?
faq.answer: Currently only the wage earner credit is applied. Child credit (KRW 150K per child), pension savings credit (up to 16.5%), and medical/donation credits must be subtracted manually from the final tax.

faq.question: Who are sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers?
faq.answer: Business owners exceeding a revenue threshold (e.g., KRW 1.5B for wholesale/retail, KRW 750M for restaurants) must attach a tax accountant's confirmation and file by June 30. Missing this triggers a penalty.

cautions: This is a simplified simulation focused on the main calculation. Real filings include detailed credits for children, donations, medical expenses, etc.
Separate-taxation items (financial income under KRW 20M, daily-wage income, etc.) are excluded from the taxable base.
Sincere-filing-confirmation taxpayers have a June 30 deadline.
Results are for reference. Verify with Hometax's 'Tax Filing Assistant' or a tax accountant.
Rates and caps may change with future law revisions. Current as of January 2026.

foreign-flat-tax.overview: Foreign workers in Korea can elect a 19% flat tax (20.9% including the 1.9% local income tax) on earned income instead of the regular progressive rates of 6–45%, under Article 18-2 of the Special Tax Treatment Control Act. This calculator compares both methods side by side from your gross salary, applying the earned-income deduction, personal deduction, and wage-earner credit on the progressive side. It reveals the real breakeven — which, contrary to the common '80 million won' claim, sits around 130–170 million won depending on your deductions. All inputs stay in your browser.
useCases: Foreign professionals (E-7 and other work visas) deciding whether to elect the flat tax at year-end settlement
High earners checking whether the 19% flat rate beats progressive taxation
Families comparing — each dependent shifts the breakeven upward
New arrivals confirming eligibility before the 2026-12-31 sunset
HR and payroll teams advising foreign employees

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter your annual gross salary (before tax, including bonuses).
Enter the number of dependents excluding yourself (spouse, children). Each adds a KRW 1.5M personal deduction on the progressive side.
Click Compare. You will see total tax under each method, the effective rate, and how much the cheaper option saves per year.
Review the progressive breakdown (taxable base, earned-income deduction, wage-earner credit) to understand why one method wins.

formula.title: Formula (Special Tax Act §18-2 vs Income Tax Act §55)
formula.body:
Flat tax = Gross salary × 19% + local 1.9% = Gross × 20.9%
  (all deductions and credits waived)

Progressive (resident basis):
  Earned-income deduction (§47, capped at KRW 20M)
  Personal deduction = KRW 1.5M × (1 + dependents)
  Taxable base = Gross − earned-income deduction − personal deduction
  Calculated tax = base × bracket rate − progressive deduction
  − Wage-earner credit (§59)
  + Local income tax 10%

Better method = whichever total is lower.

Breakeven (single filer): around KRW 137M. More dependents and more special deductions push it higher.

items.title: Mid income — gross KRW 50M
items.body: Flat: 50M × 20.9% = KRW 10.45M. Progressive: taxable base ~KRW 36.25M after deductions → about KRW 3.87M total. Progressive wins by a wide margin (~KRW 6.6M). At mid incomes the flat rate's loss of all deductions makes it far more expensive.

items.title: Breakeven — gross KRW 140M
items.body: Flat: KRW 29.26M. Progressive: about KRW 29.91M. Flat wins by only ~KRW 650K. This is the real crossover point for a single filer — far above the '80 million won' figure repeated in many guides.

items.title: High income — gross KRW 200M
items.body: Flat: KRW 41.8M. Progressive: about KRW 53.8M. Flat saves ~KRW 12M because it caps your rate at 20.9% while progressive reaches the 38% bracket.

faq.question: Who can use the 19% flat tax?
faq.answer: Foreign workers (not Korean nationals) on earned income, electing under Special Tax Act §18-2. Employees of special-related companies are excluded. It is available whether you are a tax resident or non-resident.

faq.question: Is the rate really 19%?
faq.answer: The income tax is 19%, plus a local income tax of 1.9% (10% of the income tax), for an effective 20.9%. This calculator shows both lines.

faq.question: Until when can I start using it?
faq.answer: You must begin your first employment in Korea on or before 2026-12-31. Once you qualify, the flat tax applies for 20 years from your first work day (unless the law is extended).

faq.question: Why is the breakeven so high (around KRW 130–170M)?
faq.answer: The progressive method allows the earned-income deduction, personal deductions, and the wage-earner credit, which keep effective rates low. So the flat tax only wins at high salaries with few deductions — much higher than the commonly cited KRW 80M.

faq.question: What do I give up by electing the flat tax?
faq.answer: All exemptions, deductions, and credits are waived, and even normally non-taxable allowances become taxable. If you have large deductions (medical, education, mortgage, pension), progressive is usually better.

faq.question: How do I elect the flat tax?
faq.answer: Submit a flat-tax application to your employer at year-end settlement, or to the district tax office when filing your return. You may switch your election each year.

faq.question: Does this include the 4 social insurances?
faq.answer: No. This compares income tax plus local income tax only — not national pension, health, employment, or industrial-accident insurance. It is not your net take-home pay.

cautions: Excludes the 4 social insurances and special credits (medical, education, donations, card spending) — more deductions favor progressive and raise the breakeven.
On the flat-tax side, employer-paid insurance and normally non-taxable allowances become taxable; this simplified model does not add them, so real flat-tax burden can be higher.
Resident basis. Non-residents face restricted deductions and different results.
Flat-tax new entry sunsets on 2026-12-31 unless extended by law.
Reference estimate only. Confirm with the NTS Hometax or a tax professional.

loan-calc.overview: The Korean loan calculator compares the three most common Korean repayment types — equal payment (same monthly amount), equal principal (high to low), and balloon (interest only with principal at maturity) — in one screen, with a monthly amortization schedule. The same standard formulas apply to mortgages, credit loans, jeonse-deposit loans, and auto installments. Prepayment fees, stamp duty, and DSR limits are not included.
useCases: Mortgage planning: compare total interest of equal payment vs equal principal
Credit loan: see monthly burden differences for balloon vs amortized
Jeonse deposit loan: confirm the interest-only monthly + balloon at maturity
Refinancing: enter remaining balance and new rate to see new payment
Long-term planning: track balance at 1, 5, 10 years into a 30-year mortgage

howTo.title: How to use (4 steps)
steps: Enter the loan principal in won. KRW 400M mortgage = 400000000; KRW 50M credit loan = 50000000.
Enter the loan term in years. Korean mortgages are usually 30 years, credit loans 1-5 years, jeonse loans 2 years.
Enter the annual rate (%). 4.5% = enter 4.5. For variable rates, use the current rate; simulate upward scenarios separately.

Select one of the three repayment types and click Calculate. You'll see first/last payment, total interest, total payment, and the amortization schedule (sampled every 6 months for terms over 60 months).

formula.title: Formulas (3 repayment types)
formula.body:
Equal payment: M = P × r(1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n − 1)
  P = principal, r = monthly rate (annual / 12), n = total months
  Monthly amount M is constant. Early payments lean interest-heavy, later lean principal-heavy.

Equal principal: monthly principal = P/n, monthly interest = balance × r
  First payment = P/n + P×r (largest)
  Last payment = P/n + (P/n)×r (smallest)
  Total interest is ~4-5% less than equal payment.

Balloon: monthly = P × r (interest only)
  Last payment = P × r + P (full principal at maturity)
  Highest total interest but lightest monthly burden. Common for Korean credit and jeonse loans.

items.title: Example 1: KRW 400M / 30 years / 4.5% / equal payment
items.body: Monthly rate 0.375%, 360 payments. Monthly amount ~KRW 2,026,920. Total payment ~KRW 729.7M, total interest ~KRW 329.7M — nearly equal to the principal over 30 years.

items.title: Example 2: Same terms, equal principal comparison
items.body: First payment KRW 2,611,111 (principal 1.11M + interest 1.5M), last payment KRW 1,115,278. Total interest ~KRW 270.7M — about KRW 59M less than equal payment. But the first 5 years cost ~KRW 500K more per month.

items.title: Example 3: KRW 50M / 3 years / 6% / balloon
items.body: Monthly interest = 50M × 6%/12 = KRW 250,000. 36 months of interest only, then KRW 50M + final KRW 250K at maturity. Total interest KRW 9M. Light monthly but the maturity lump sum needs a clear repayment source.

faq.question: Equal payment vs equal principal — which is better?
faq.answer: By total interest, equal principal saves about 4-5%. But the first 5-10 years carry a much heavier monthly burden. If early cash flow is tight, choose equal payment; if you have early room and want long-term savings, choose equal principal.

faq.question: Why is balloon repayment common in Korea?
faq.answer: Credit loans and jeonse-deposit loans run short (1-3 years) for a specific large need. Minimizing monthly principal payments and repaying at maturity from another source (deposit refund, sale proceeds) fits this pattern.

faq.question: How are prepayment fees handled?
faq.answer: This calculator does not include them. Korean prepayment fees are typically 0.5-1.5% of the outstanding balance and apply within the first 3 years. When refinancing, subtract fees from interest savings to see the real benefit.

faq.question: Where do I check the DSR (debt service ratio) limit?
faq.answer: DSR is annual principal+interest divided by annual income, capped at 40% for banks and 50% for secondary lenders. Multiply this calculator's monthly payment by 12 and divide by your annual income, summing across all debts.

faq.question: How do I handle variable-rate loans?
faq.answer: Variable rates reset every 6 or 12 months. This calculator assumes a single rate, so simulate upward scenarios (e.g., +1% in 1 year) conservatively by re-running with the adjusted rate.

faq.question: Why do first and last payments differ?
faq.answer: Equal payment is constant; equal principal's last payment is ~30-40% smaller as the balance shrinks; balloon's monthly is the same but the last payment adds the full principal.

faq.question: How much does early repayment save?
faq.answer: Paying off a 30-year mortgage at year 10 eliminates years 11-30 of interest entirely. Prepayment fees typically apply within 5 years (0.5-1.5%), then disappear. Early repayment is a powerful saving tool.

cautions: Prepayment fees, stamp duty, and mortgage registration fees are not included.
The DSR limit (40% for banks) must be checked separately.
Variable rates assume a single rate — simulate upward scenarios separately.
Monthly rate r = annual rate / 12 (Korean standard simple-rate division).
Results are for planning. Actual terms are confirmed by the lender.

rent-cap.overview: The Korean rent cap calculator verifies whether a proposed rent increase at lease renewal complies with Article 8 of the Housing Lease Act Enforcement Decree, which caps annual increases at 5%. For mixed deposit + monthly rent leases, the calculator uses the conversion rate (Article 9, BoK base rate + 2%) to compute an equivalent deposit before applying the 5% cap. Tenants can verify whether a landlord's proposal exceeds the legal limit; landlords can simulate maximum deposit-only or rent-only increases within the cap.
useCases: Tenant: instantly verify whether the landlord's renewal proposal exceeds 5%
Landlord: simulate which side to increase (deposit or rent) within the cap
Real estate agent: provide objective limits during renewal negotiations
Deposit ↔ rent conversion: compare options on an equivalent-deposit basis
Dispute prevention: establish a legal reference when contesting an over-cap notice

howTo.title: How to use (5 steps)
steps: Enter the current deposit and current monthly rent in won. Pure jeonse: rent = 0. Pure monthly: deposit = 0.
Enter the landlord's proposed deposit and proposed monthly rent. Either or both can be changed.
Confirm the conversion rate. As of 2026-05 it's about 5.0% (BoK base 3.0% + 2%). Update if the base rate changes.
The cap defaults to 5%. Some local ordinances may set a lower cap (e.g., 4% in certain districts) — adjust if applicable.
Click Verify. You'll see the increase rate, pass/fail status, excess or remaining amount, and the maximum deposit-only or rent-only increase that would stay within the cap.

formula.title: Equivalent deposit formula (Article 9)
formula.body:
Equivalent deposit = deposit + (monthly rent × 12 / conversion rate)
  Example: deposit KRW 50M + monthly KRW 500K at 5% conversion
     = 50M + (500K × 12 / 0.05) = 50M + 120M = KRW 170M

5% cap = current equivalent deposit × 1.05
  Example: 170M × 1.05 = KRW 178.5M

If the proposed equivalent deposit ≤ cap → within limit
If above cap → tenant may exercise renewal rights and reject the excess

items.title: Example 1: KRW 500M jeonse → landlord proposes KRW 530M (+6%)
items.body: Equivalent deposit = KRW 500M (no rent). Cap = KRW 525M. Proposal = KRW 530M → KRW 5M over. Tenant exercising renewal rights accepts up to KRW 525M. If refused, the dispute mediation committee can be petitioned.

items.title: Example 2: KRW 100M + KRW 800K monthly → landlord wants KRW 120M + KRW 800K
items.body: Current equivalent = 100M + (800K × 12 / 0.05) = 100M + 192M = KRW 292M. Cap = KRW 306.6M. Proposed equivalent = 120M + 192M = KRW 312M → KRW 5.4M over. Within cap, deposit could go up to KRW 114.6M (rent unchanged).

items.title: Example 3: Rent KRW 500K → KRW 530K (+6%)
items.body: Deposit KRW 50M + rent KRW 500K. Equivalent KRW 170M. Cap KRW 178.5M. New equivalent (rent KRW 530K) = 50M + (530K × 12 / 0.05) = 50M + 127.2M = KRW 177.2M < KRW 178.5M → within cap. A 6% rent increase translates to only ~4.2% on the equivalent basis.

faq.question: Does the 5% cap apply to all leases?
faq.answer: Only when the tenant exercises the renewal right (6 to 1 months before lease end). It does not apply to new contracts, implied renewals, or when the landlord has a legitimate refusal reason (such as personal occupancy).

faq.question: Can the landlord refuse renewal claiming personal occupancy?
faq.answer: Yes, if the landlord or their direct family will actually live there. However, if they falsely refuse and lease to someone else within 2 years, the tenant can claim damages.

faq.question: How many times can renewal rights be exercised?
faq.answer: Once after the initial contract, granting an additional 2 years. So 2 years initial + 2 years renewal = 4 years total guaranteed by law.

faq.question: How is the conversion rate set?
faq.answer: Article 9 sets it at 'BoK base rate + 2%'. If the base rate is 3.0%, the conversion rate is 5.0%. Check the Bank of Korea's website for the current base rate and update the calculator.

faq.question: Can I raise only the deposit and keep rent the same?
faq.answer: Yes. The calculator shows the maximum deposit when rent is unchanged. A deposit increase of 6-7% can still comply if the equivalent-basis change stays under 5%.

faq.question: I already received an over-cap notice — what now?
faq.answer: 1) Send a formal renewal-rights notice in writing (certified mail). 2) State your acceptance of the within-cap amount. 3) If the landlord refuses, petition the housing dispute mediation committee.

faq.question: Can local ordinances set a lower cap?
faq.answer: Yes — Article 8 defines '5% or less', allowing local ordinances to lower it. Some districts apply 4% or less. Check your local government's ordinance.

cautions: This tool verifies the renewal-time cap. New contracts are not subject to the 5% rule.
The conversion rate varies with the BoK base rate — enter the latest value.
Local ordinances may set a stricter cap. Check your district's rules.
The landlord's legitimate refusal reasons (personal occupancy, demolition, tenant breach) override renewal rights.
Results are for reference. Legal force comes from the dispute committee or court decision.

area-convert.overview: The Korean area converter handles the three units most used in Korean real estate, interior, and construction work: pyeong (평), square meters (㎡), and Japanese-style ja² (尺²). Using the exact ratio 1 pyeong = 3,600/1,089 ≈ 3.30579 ㎡, it converts all three units instantly and offers presets for common Korean real estate sizes (59㎡, 84㎡, 114㎡) and pyeong values (30평, 50평).
useCases: Comparing Korean apartments: how many pyeong is an 84㎡ unit (= 25.41 pyeong)
Interior estimates: convert pyeong-based prices to per-㎡ unit prices
Architectural drawings: convert Japanese/old-Korean ja² units
Lease contracts: convert the ㎡ in official registration to pyeong
Vacancy allocation: convert pyeong-based rent to ㎡-based ratio

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the area into any one of the three unit boxes (pyeong, ㎡, ja²). The other two units update automatically.
Preset buttons (59㎡, 84㎡, 114㎡, 30평, 50평) apply common Korean real estate sizes with one click.
Results show pyeong to two decimals, ㎡ and ja² to two decimals, plus a '0.5-pyeong rounded' value matching real estate listing conventions.

formula.title: Conversion formulas (Korean standard)
formula.body:
1 pyeong = 6 ja × 6 ja = 36 ja² = 3,600/1,089 ㎡ ≈ 3.30579 ㎡
1 ja = 10/33 m ≈ 0.30303 m

pyeong → ㎡  : pyeong × 3.30579
㎡ → pyeong  : ㎡ / 3.30579 ≈ ㎡ × 0.3025
pyeong → ja² : pyeong × 36
ja² → ㎡     : ja² × (10/33)² ≈ ja² × 0.0918

Korean real estate sizes:
  59㎡  ≈ 17.84 pyeong (the '18-pyeong' type, exclusive area)
  84㎡  ≈ 25.41 pyeong (the '25-pyeong' type)
  114㎡ ≈ 34.49 pyeong (the '34-pyeong' type)

items.title: Example 1: Exact pyeong of an 84㎡ apartment
items.body: 84 / 3.30579 = 25.408 pyeong. Rounded to 0.5 pyeong = 25.5 pyeong. Real estate listings of '25 pyeong' or '26 pyeong' usually refer to supply area (exclusive + common) or rounded conventions.

items.title: Example 2: 30 pyeong construction in ㎡
items.body: 30 × 3.30579 = 99.17 ㎡. An interior estimate of 'KRW 1M per pyeong' for 30 pyeong is KRW 30M, which is KRW ~302,500 per ㎡.

items.title: Example 3: Japanese ja² drawing
items.body: A '108 ja²' label on a drawing → 108 × 0.0918 ≈ 9.91 ㎡ ≈ 3 pyeong. Useful when reading Japanese or old-Korean drawings.

faq.question: Exactly how many ㎡ is 1 pyeong?
faq.answer: 1 pyeong = 3,600 / 1,089 = exactly 3.30578512... ㎡. The 3.3 ㎡ rough estimate is common but accumulates errors at larger sizes. This calculator uses the exact ratio.

faq.question: What is the difference between exclusive, supply, and contract area?
faq.answer: Exclusive = livable space (rooms, living, bath). Supply = exclusive + shared residential (corridors, stairs). Contract = supply + other shared (parking, management). '84 ㎡' usually refers to exclusive area, while '25 pyeong type' refers to supply area roughly 80-90 ㎡.

faq.question: Why is pyeong still in use?
faq.answer: Korea has required metric units since 1961, but real estate and interior conventions remain pyeong-based. Official deeds and contracts use ㎡, while listings and casual use prefer pyeong.

faq.question: Where is ja² (尺²) used?
faq.answer: Old-Korean drawings, Japanese architecture, traditional medicine, and traditional furniture sizing. 1 pyeong = 36 ja², so conversion is straightforward.

faq.question: Why show a '0.5-pyeong rounded' value separately?
faq.answer: Real estate listings typically round to 0.5 pyeong (e.g., 25.4 → 25.5). The calculator shows both the exact value and the listing-style rounded value.

faq.question: Can I use this for building/floor area ratios?
faq.answer: No — this tool only handles unit conversion. Korean building coverage ratio (building area / lot area) and floor area ratio (gross floor area / lot area) follow zoning-specific limits and require building code calculations.

cautions: 1 pyeong = 3.30579 ㎡ exactly. The 3.3 ㎡ shortcut accumulates errors at large areas.
Real estate listings mix exclusive and supply area. Always verify the ㎡ on the official registration.
Ja² is an old-Korean/Japanese unit. Confirm the drawing's origin when mixing units.
Only ㎡ has legal force in official contracts and deeds. Pyeong is for everyday convenience.

percent-calc.overview: The percent calculator handles the five most common percent operations on one screen — B% of A (discount/portion), A increased by B%, A decreased by B%, change rate from A to B, and reverse calculation when the result of a B% increase equals A. Use it for shopping discounts, VAT separation, salary raises, profit margins, and investment returns.
useCases: Shopping: actual paid amount after a 20% discount
Salary: change rate from last year's KRW 50M to this year's KRW 55M (10%)
VAT split: separate KRW 110K (VAT-inclusive) into KRW 100K supply + KRW 10K VAT
Margin: cost KRW 10K → sale KRW 15K (50% markup, 33.3% margin)
Investment: KRW 100M invested → KRW 120M returned = 20% gain

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Pick one of the five modes: B% of A, A increased by B%, A decreased by B%, change rate from A to B, or reverse (find original from increased value).
Enter the two values required by the chosen mode. The result updates instantly — no Calculate button needed.
Preset buttons for VAT (10%) and common discounts (10/20/30/50%) speed up common cases.

formula.title: Five mode formulas
formula.body:
1) B% of A          : A × B/100
   Example: 20% of 50,000 = 10,000

2) A increased by B%: A × (1 + B/100)
   Example: 30,000 + 10% = 33,000

3) A decreased by B%: A × (1 − B/100)
   Example: 30,000 − 10% = 27,000

4) A → B change rate: (B − A) / A × 100
   Example: 100 → 130 = +30%

5) Reverse from B% increase: A / (1 + B/100)
   Example: VAT-inclusive 11,000 → 11,000 / 1.10 = 10,000 (supply price)

items.title: Example 1: 30% off KRW 89,000
items.body: Decreased mode → A=89,000, B=30. Result = 89,000 × 0.70 = 62,300. Discount amount = 26,700.

items.title: Example 2: Salary KRW 50M → KRW 54M raise rate
items.body: Change rate mode → A=50,000,000, B=54,000,000. Rate = (54M − 50M) / 50M × 100 = 8%. Net pay differs after taxes and insurance — check separately.

faq.question: Does +10% then −10% return to the original?
faq.answer: No. 100 → +10% = 110 → −10% = 99. A 1% loss occurs because the percentages apply to different base values (100 vs 110).

faq.question: How do I split VAT (10%)?
faq.answer: Inclusive ÷ 1.1 gives the supply price; the difference is VAT. Example: 11,000 inclusive → 10,000 supply + 1,000 VAT. Multiplying inclusive by 10% is the wrong calculation.

faq.question: Are margin and markup the same?
faq.answer: No. Cost 10K → sale 15K means 50% markup (against cost) but 33.3% margin (against sale price). Markup measures cost-to-price; margin measures profit-to-price.

faq.question: Is the percent calculation the same as compound vs simple interest?
faq.answer: Simple is straight addition each period; compound applies the rate to the new balance each period. Single-rate increases here are simple. For multi-period returns, use the compound interest calculator.

faq.question: I see rounding differences in discount calculations
faq.answer: The calculator shows two decimals. Real charges round to the nearest won (by card-network policy), so 1-2 won differences can appear. Verify against the actual receipt for large amounts.

faq.question: Can the change rate be negative?
faq.answer: Yes. 100 → 80 = (80−100)/100 = −20%. The calculator shows signed change rates automatically.

cautions: +B% then −B% does not return to the original (100 → 110 → 99).
Split VAT via 'inclusive ÷ 1.1', not 'inclusive × 10%'.
Markup (vs cost) and margin (vs sale price) are different ratios.
Card-network and tax-office rounding rules may differ — verify on actual receipts for large amounts.

gravel-calc.overview: The gravel calculator computes the volume (m³) and weight (tons) of gravel, sand, or crushed stone from area and depth. It applies per-material unit weights, includes a compaction allowance, and tells you the number of 25kg bags and 1-ton bulk bags to order. Use it for driveways, parking, drainage layers, and landscaping.
useCases: Estimate gravel for yard/driveway paving
Aggregate for drainage and base layers
Landscaping gravel/decomposed-granite order
Crushed-stone base before concrete
Garden sand/gravel purchase amount

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the area (m²) to cover, computed as length × width.
Enter depth (cm). Gravel paving 5-10cm, compacted base 10-20cm is typical.
Select material and compaction allowance (usually 20%) to see volume, weight, and bag counts.

formula.title: Volume & weight formula
formula.body:
Volume (m³) = area (m²) × depth (m)
Order volume = volume × (1 + compaction)
Weight (tons) = order volume × unit weight

Unit weight (t/m³, loose):
  gravel 1.5, sand 1.6, crushed 1.5, soil 1.4, mixed 1.55

25kg bags = weight(kg) / 25
1-ton bulk bags = ceil(weight in tons)

items.title: Example 1: Yard 20m² × 10cm gravel
items.body: Volume 2m³, +20% compaction → 2.4m³. Weight 2.4 × 1.5 = 3.6 tons. 144 25kg bags or 4 bulk bags.

items.title: Example 2: Driveway drainage 30m² × 15cm crushed
items.body: Volume 4.5m³ × 1.2 = 5.4m³. Crushed 1.5 → 8.1 tons. Compacted bases need to be laid thicker.

items.title: Example 3: Garden sand 10m² × 5cm
items.body: Volume 0.5m³ × 1.2 = 0.6m³. Sand 1.6 → 0.96 tons. About 39 bags.

faq.question: Why a compaction allowance?
faq.answer: Gravel/aggregate loses ~20-30% volume when compacted. To reach the target depth after compaction, you must order extra, so a compaction allowance (default 20%) is added.

faq.question: How is unit weight determined?
faq.answer: It varies by material and moisture. This tool uses general loose-dry values (gravel 1.5, sand 1.6 t/m³). Wet sand is heavier and may differ from the estimate.

faq.question: Bags or bulk bags?
faq.answer: Small amounts (under 1 ton) are easy in 25kg bags; large amounts are cheaper per unit in 1-ton bulk bags. The tool shows both — choose based on transport and storage.

faq.question: What depth is right for gravel?
faq.answer: Decorative finish 3-5cm, walkways 5-8cm, vehicle driveways 8-10cm, compacted base 10-20cm. Heavier loads need thicker layers.

faq.question: Rubble vs crushed stone?
faq.answer: Rubble is broken natural stone (uneven sizes); crushed stone is aggregate crushed to a set size. Bases mix rubble + crushed; finishes use uniform crushed/gravel.

faq.question: Is geotextile separate?
faq.answer: Yes. Weed-barrier/separation geotextile under gravel is calculated by area (m²) separately. This tool computes aggregate only.

cautions: Unit weights are loose-dry general values — vary with moisture.
20% compaction is the default; adjust for compaction strength.
Wet sand/aggregate is heavier.
Geotextile and edging are separate.
Verify unit weight with the supplier for accurate ordering.

deck-calc.overview: The deck and fence calculator computes deck board count and joists from area, and fence posts, rails, and pickets from length. It reflects construction variables like board gap and joist spacing and includes a waste allowance, ready for woodworking and landscaping DIY orders.
useCases: Estimate deck boards for a build
Calculate joist count
Garden fence posts and pickets
Fence material quote
Woodworking DIY ordering

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Choose the calculation type (deck/fence).
For deck: area, board width/length, gap. For fence: length, post spacing, picket spacing.
See the boards/posts needed and joists/rails/pickets. Includes 10% waste.

formula.title: Material quantity formula
formula.body:
[Deck]
Effective board width = width + gap
Boards = area / (effective width × length) × (1 + waste)
Joists = deck side length / joist spacing + 1

[Fence]
Posts = ceil(length / post spacing) + 1
Rails = sections × 2 (top/bottom)
Pickets = length / picket spacing

items.title: Example 1: 20m² deck, 140mm board 3.6m
items.body: Effective width 145mm, 0.522m² per board. 20/0.522 = 38.3 × 1.1 = 43 boards. Joists: 5m side at 400mm = 14.

items.title: Example 2: 20m fence, 2m post spacing
items.body: 10 sections + 1 = 11 posts. 20 rails (2 per section). At 100mm picket spacing, 200 pickets.

items.title: Example 3: Narrow boards (90mm)
items.body: Narrower effective width needs more boards for the same area — denser look and higher cost.

faq.question: Why a board gap?
faq.answer: Wood expands/contracts with moisture and needs drainage/ventilation, so leave a 3-6mm gap between boards. The gap adds to effective width, affecting board count.

faq.question: What joist spacing is right?
faq.answer: Standard deck boards use 400mm (16in) joist spacing; heavy loads or thin boards use 300mm. Tighter spacing is stronger but needs more joists.

faq.question: Is 10% waste enough?
faq.answer: Enough for simple rectangular decks; diagonals, stairs, and complex shapes warrant 15-20%. It covers cutting loss and defective boards.

faq.question: Why posts = sections + 1?
faq.answer: Each gap between posts is a section, and you need posts at both ends, so one more than the section count. A 10-section fence needs 11 posts. Corners and gates may need extra posts.

faq.question: Pressure-treated vs composite (WPC)?
faq.answer: Pressure-treated is cheaper but needs periodic oiling; composite (WPC) is pricier with less maintenance. The count is the same but board dimensions may differ.

faq.question: Are deck footings calculated?
faq.answer: This tool computes boards, joists, and fence materials only. Deck footings (concrete piers, posts) require separate design based on area and load.

cautions: 10% waste default — complex shapes 15-20%.
Standard joist spacing 400mm; thin boards 300mm.
Board gap affects the count.
Deck footings (piers, posts) require separate design.
Check pressure-treated vs composite board dimensions.

btu-calc.overview: The BTU / AC sizing calculator computes the cooling capacity (BTU) and Korean AC 'pyeong type' from room area. It adjusts for ceiling height, sun exposure, occupants, and kitchen, and converts to kW — helping you avoid choosing an AC that's too small or too large.
useCases: Confirm AC capacity before buying
Pick the right 'pyeong type' by room area
Compare living room, bedroom, kitchen capacity
Adjust for sunny or crowded spaces
Convert BTU ↔ pyeong type

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter room area (m²) and ceiling height.
Select sun exposure (low/normal/high) and occupants. More sun/people means more capacity.
Check kitchen if applicable (cooking heat). See recommended BTU, Korean pyeong type, and kW.

formula.title: BTU sizing formula
formula.body:
Base cooling = area (m²) × 600 BTU/m²

Adjustments:
  Ceiling over 2.7m scales proportionally
  Sun high ×1.1, low ×0.9
  Occupants over 2: +600 BTU each
  Kitchen +4,000 BTU

Korean pyeong type: BTU / 1,440 (1 pyeong ≈ 1,440 BTU)
kW: BTU × 0.000293

items.title: Example 1: 20m² bedroom (normal)
items.body: 20 × 600 = 12,000 BTU ≈ 8.3 pyeong type. Suitable for a standard bedroom wall/stand AC.

items.title: Example 2: 30m² living room, high sun, 4 people
items.body: 30 × 600 × 1.1 + occupant adjustment = ~21,000 BTU ≈ 14.6 pyeong type. Large rooms scale up with sun and people.

items.title: Example 3: Space with kitchen
items.body: Cooking heat adds +4,000 BTU. Same area, but kitchens need more capacity.

faq.question: What is BTU?
faq.answer: British Thermal Unit, a heating/cooling capacity unit — the heat removable (cooling) or supplied (heating) per hour. Higher means more powerful; Korea also labels it as 'pyeong type'.

faq.question: Is bigger always better?
faq.answer: No. An oversized unit reaches the set temperature quickly and cycles on/off frequently, removes less humidity, wastes electricity, and risks failure. Right-sizing to the area is most efficient.

faq.question: How do I convert to pyeong type?
faq.answer: One Korean AC pyeong type is about 1,440 BTU (~363 kcal/h). E.g., 12,000 BTU ÷ 1,440 ≈ 8.3 pyeong type. Manufacturer ratings often build in a margin over actual area.

faq.question: Why does sun exposure matter?
faq.answer: South/west-facing rooms with strong sun gain radiant heat, raising the cooling load. High sun adds +10%, shaded rooms −10%. Large windows and high floors also increase the load.

faq.question: Why size up for kitchens?
faq.answer: Stoves and ovens generate a lot of heat. Kitchens and adjacent spaces need about +4,000 BTU for adequate cooling.

faq.question: What if insulation is poor?
faq.answer: Poor insulation/windows raise the cooling load, so add ~20% over this estimate to be safe. Conversely, new high-insulation builds have margin.

cautions: Based on area × 600 BTU/m² plus adjustments. ±20% by insulation and windows.
Manufacturer pyeong ratings tend to build in margin.
Oversizing causes short cycling, poor dehumidification, wasted power.
Kitchens, high floors, large windows need more capacity.
For exact sizing, get an installer's on-site assessment.

car-acquisition-tax.overview: The Korean car acquisition tax calculator computes the tax from a vehicle's purchase price. It applies 7% for passenger cars, 4% for light cars, and 5% for vans/cargo, with up to KRW 1.4M eco-car discount and exemption for light cars under KRW 500K. Check the actual tax before registering a new or used vehicle.
useCases: Estimate acquisition tax before buying a new car
Calculate tax on a used-car purchase
Check EV/hydrogen-car discounts
Determine light-car exemption
Plan total vehicle purchase cost

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the acquisition price (taxable base, excl. VAT). New cars use ex-factory price; used cars the higher of actual price/assessed value.
Select vehicle type (passenger/light/eco/van-cargo) to apply the rate and discount.
See the tax after the eco discount. Bond purchase costs are separate.

formula.title: Acquisition tax formula (Local Tax Act)
formula.body:
Tax = price × rate
  Passenger: 7%
  Light (≤1000cc): 4% (exempt if ≤ KRW 500K)
  Van/cargo: 5%

Eco (EV/hydrogen): up to KRW 1.4M discount

e.g. passenger 30M × 7% = 2.1M KRW
  eco 50M × 7% = 3.5M − 1.4M = 2.1M KRW

items.title: Example 1: Passenger car KRW 30M
items.body: 30M × 7% = 2.1M KRW. Bond purchase (by region/price) and stamp fees are added separately.

items.title: Example 2: Light car KRW 10M
items.body: 10M × 4% = 400K, but under 500K so exempt. Tax 0. A light-car benefit.

items.title: Example 3: EV KRW 50M
items.body: 50M × 7% = 3.5M − 1.4M eco discount = 2.1M KRW. KRW 1.4M cheaper than the equivalent gas car.

faq.question: Are acquisition and registration tax different?
faq.answer: Registration tax was merged into acquisition tax in 2011, so only 'acquisition tax' is levied now. People say '취등록세' but the actual item is acquisition tax.

faq.question: Why are bond costs separate?
faq.answer: Vehicle registration requires buying regional development bonds — that's a bond purchase, not a tax. Selling them immediately at a discount means you bear only part of the cost. It varies by region and price.

faq.question: What's the basis for used cars?
faq.answer: The higher of the actual transaction price and the local-tax assessed value (by model year). Declaring too low triggers assessment-based taxation.

faq.question: Is there a multi-child discount?
faq.answer: From 2025, households with 2+ children under 18 get a full exemption (3 children) or 50% (2 children) on 6-seat-or-fewer passenger cars. This calculator doesn't include it — verify separately.

faq.question: Until when is the eco discount valid?
faq.answer: The EV/hydrogen acquisition tax discount (up to KRW 1.4M) is extended/reduced by policy. Check the current cap at purchase.

faq.question: When do I pay acquisition tax?
faq.answer: File and pay within 60 days of acquisition (registration). Usually handled together with vehicle registration; late payment incurs penalties.

cautions: Local Tax Act basis. Bond purchase and stamp fees are separate.
Used cars use the higher of actual price/assessed value.
2025 multi-child discount not included — verify separately.
Eco-discount cap changes with policy.
File/pay within 60 days of acquisition.

car-tax.overview: The Korean annual car tax calculator computes the yearly tax from engine displacement and vehicle age. It applies per-cc rates (≤1000cc 80, ≤1600cc 140, >1600cc 200 KRW) plus 30% education tax, age reduction (5%/year from year 3, up to 50%), and a flat EV rate (KRW 130K). It also shows the half-year payments due in June and December.
useCases: Check annual car tax on your vehicle
Compare ownership cost before buying
See age-based tax reduction
Check EV car tax
See base tax before the annual prepayment discount

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter displacement (cc), shown on the vehicle registration.
Enter vehicle age (years); reduction applies from year 3.
Check the EV box for the flat KRW 130K rate. See the annual total and half-year payments.

formula.title: Car tax formula (non-commercial passenger)
formula.body:
Car tax = displacement × per-cc rate
  ≤1000cc: 80, ≤1600cc: 140, >1600cc: 200 KRW

Education tax = car tax × 30%
Annual total = car tax + education tax

Age reduction: 5%/year from year 3, max 50% (year 12+)
EV: flat KRW 130K (non-commercial passenger)

e.g. 1600cc = 1600 × 140 = 224,000 + edu 67,200 = 291,200 KRW

items.title: Example 1: 1600cc new car
items.body: 1600 × 140 = 224,000 + 30% edu 67,200 = 291,200 KRW/year. ~145,600 each in June and December.

items.title: Example 2: 2000cc, 5 years old
items.body: 2000 × 200 = 400K base. 15% age reduction at year 5 → ~340K + edu. Older = less tax.

items.title: Example 3: EV
items.body: Flat KRW 130K regardless of displacement + 39,000 edu = 169K/year. Far cheaper than a large gas car.

faq.question: Why is car tax based on displacement?
faq.answer: Current car tax uses displacement (cc) as the base. Larger displacement = higher per-cc rate = more tax. Since it's not price-based, an expensive small car can pay less than a large one.

faq.question: How does age reduction work?
faq.answer: From year 3 after registration, 5% reduction per year, reaching up to 50% at year 12. Older cars carry a lighter tax.

faq.question: Is there a prepayment discount?
faq.answer: Yes. Paying the full year in January gives ~7% off (2024 basis, shrinking yearly). This calculator shows the base before discount.

faq.question: Why is EV tax flat?
faq.answer: EVs have no displacement, so a flat KRW 130K applies (non-commercial passenger). Far below a large gas car (2000cc 400K+) — one of the EV ownership perks.

faq.question: What is the education tax?
faq.answer: A surtax on car tax — 30% goes to regional education funding. It's combined on the car tax bill.

faq.question: When do I pay car tax?
faq.answer: Twice a year — June (Jan-Jun) and December (Jul-Dec) in halves. With annual prepayment in January you pay the discounted full year at once.

cautions: Non-commercial passenger basis. Commercial/van/cargo use a different system.
Prepayment discount (~7%) not included — base tax shown.
EV flat rate is KRW 130K for non-commercial passenger.
Age reduction starts from year 3 after registration.
30% education tax is added to car tax.

gift-money.overview: The Korean gift money calculator recommends appropriate amounts for wedding (축의금) and funeral (부의금) cash gifts based on relationship and attendance. There's no fixed rule, but customary ranges exist by closeness — acquaintance, colleague, friend, close friend, relative — plus the custom of giving one tier higher when attending (to cover the meal) and the preference for odd numbers.
useCases: When unsure how much wedding gift money to give
Check appropriate funeral money
Set a baseline for a colleague's event
See the difference between attending and not
Guide for foreigners new to Korean ceremony customs

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Choose the occasion (wedding/funeral).
Pick your relationship (acquaintance/colleague/friend/close friend/relative).
For weddings, check attendance — the recommendation adjusts to cover the meal.

formula.title: Recommended amounts (custom)
formula.body:
Wedding (10,000 KRW units):
  acquaintance 5 / colleague 5-10 / friend 5-10 / close friend 10-20 / relative 10-30
Funeral:
  acquaintance 3-5 / colleague 5-10 / friend 5-10 / close friend 10-20 / relative 10-30

Give one tier higher when attending (meal cost).
Odd amounts preferred (30/50/70/100K) — even numbers imply 'division' (100K is the exception).

items.title: Example 1: Colleague's wedding, attending
items.body: Colleague base 50K, but attending (meal ~50K) suggests 100K. Custom is to give more than the meal cost.

items.title: Example 2: Close friend's wedding
items.body: Close friend range 100-200K. Decide based on the friendship and future reciprocity at your own wedding.

items.title: Example 3: Boss's parent's funeral
items.body: Colleague/boss funeral money 50-100K. Sometimes the team contributes together. Attendance matters more than the amount.

faq.question: Should the amount be an odd number?
faq.answer: Korea prefers odd amounts (30/50/70K). Even numbers imply 'division (separation)' and are avoided, but 100K is an exception ('completeness'). It's not an absolute rule.

faq.question: Can I give less if I don't attend?
faq.answer: Attending incurs a meal cost (~50K), so when not attending, the amount minus the meal (e.g., colleague 50K) is fine. When attending, custom is to exceed the meal cost.

faq.question: I'm a foreigner invited to a Korean wedding
faq.answer: 50-100K is safe depending on relationship. Write your name on the envelope and hand it at the reception desk; use crisp new bills in a white envelope. Funerals use white envelopes; weddings white/plain too.

faq.question: Do wedding and funeral amounts differ?
faq.answer: Similar, but funeral money tends to be slightly lower. For funerals, attendance to share grief matters more than the amount.

faq.question: If the company contributes, do I still give?
faq.answer: When a team/department jointly gives a wreath or condolence money, individual extra is optional by closeness. Close ones may add a little separately.

faq.question: What do I write on the envelope?
faq.answer: For weddings, 祝結婚/祝華婚 on the front; for funerals, 賻儀/謹弔, with your name and affiliation on the back. Korean ('축 결혼' / 'condolences') is also fine.

cautions: No fixed rule — a reference varying by relationship, region, prices.
Give one tier higher when attending (meal cost).
Odd numbers preferred, but 100K is the exception.
For funerals, attendance matters more than the amount.
Use new bills in a white/plain envelope.

due-date.overview: The due date calculator estimates your delivery date from the last menstrual period (LMP). Based on Naegele's rule (LMP + 280 days), it shows the current pregnancy week, trimester, progress, and days to go, and auto-adjusts for cycles other than 28 days.
useCases: Calculate the due date after confirming pregnancy
Check current pregnancy week and trimester
Plan prenatal checkups and maternity leave
Adjusted due date for irregular cycles
Track pregnancy progress and D-day

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP).
Enter your average cycle length. Default is 28 days; otherwise ovulation timing is adjusted.
See the due date, current week/trimester, progress, and days remaining.

formula.title: Naegele's rule
formula.body:
EDD = LMP + 280 days (40 weeks)
  = LMP + 1 year − 3 months + 7 days

Pregnancy weeks = (today − LMP) / 7
Trimester: 1st 0-13 wks, 2nd 14-27, 3rd 28+

Cycle adjustment: shift EDD by (cycle − 28) days
  e.g. 35-day cycle → EDD 7 days later

items.title: Example 1: LMP Jan 1 (28-day cycle)
items.body: Jan 1 + 280 days = Oct 8 due date. Based on the 40-week full term.

items.title: Example 2: 20 weeks pregnant
items.body: 140 days after LMP = 20 weeks = 2nd trimester. Anatomy scan and sex determination period. 50% progress.

items.title: Example 3: 35-day cycle
items.body: Later ovulation shifts the due date 7 days past the 28-day estimate. Cycle adjustment gives a more accurate date.

faq.question: Will I deliver exactly on the due date?
faq.answer: It's an estimate. Full term spans 37-42 weeks, and only ~5% deliver on the exact due date. Most deliver within ±2 weeks.

faq.question: Is it accurate with irregular cycles?
faq.answer: Naegele's rule assumes a regular 28-day cycle. Irregular cycles increase error, so an ultrasound (fetal-size) due date is more accurate. This tool supports cycle adjustment but is for reference.

faq.question: When does pregnancy week counting start?
faq.answer: The first day of LMP is week 0 day 0. Actual conception is ~2 weeks later, but since exact ovulation is unknown, LMP is the standard. So 'week 4' is around the expected period date.

faq.question: How are trimesters divided?
faq.answer: 1st 0-13 weeks (organ formation, nausea), 2nd 14-27 (stable, movement), 3rd 28+ (rapid growth, term prep). Each has different tests and precautions.

faq.question: It differs from the ultrasound date — which is right?
faq.answer: The early ultrasound (7-12 weeks) date is most accurate. If it differs from LMP by 1+ weeks, the ultrasound date is usually adopted. This tool estimates from LMP, so prioritize your OB-GYN's confirmed date.

faq.question: Same due date for twins?
faq.answer: Twins/multiples tend to be born earlier (usually before 37 weeks). This calculator assumes a singleton, so follow medical guidance for multiples.

cautions: Naegele's rule (LMP+280) estimate — ultrasound date is more accurate.
Full term spans 37-42 weeks; ~5% deliver on the due date.
Irregular cycles increase error.
Twins/multiples tend to be earlier (separate).
Confirm the exact date via OB-GYN ultrasound.

net-salary.overview: The Korean salary take-home calculator computes your actual monthly pay after the 4 insurances and income tax are deducted from your annual salary. It reflects national pension, health, long-term care, and employment insurance, plus income and local tax, and accounts for dependents, children, and the non-taxable allowance. See gross-to-net at a glance before a job change or salary negotiation.
useCases: Compare take-home before a job change or negotiation
See the real monthly increase from a raise
Understand tax differences by dependents and children
Accurate pay reflecting the non-taxable meal allowance
Grasp the gap between gross salary and net pay

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter your annual salary (gross, KRW). Base salary excluding bonuses is typical.
Enter dependents (incl. self) and children under 20 for personal and child deductions.
Enter the monthly non-taxable amount (meal allowance, default KRW 200,000), excluded from both insurance and tax.

formula.title: Deduction items (2026)
formula.body:
Monthly gross = annual / 12
Taxable = monthly gross − non-taxable

4 insurances (employee):
  National pension 4.5% (income cap KRW 6.17M)
  Health 3.545%
  Long-term care = health × 12.95%
  Employment 0.9%

Income tax: earned-income deduction → personal/pension deductions → progressive rate
  − wage earner credit − child credit → /12
Local income tax = income tax × 10%

Take-home = gross − insurances − income tax − local tax

items.title: Example 1: KRW 36M (monthly 3M)
items.body: Insurances ~270K plus small income tax. Take-home around 2.7M. Low salaries have lower deduction rates, so a higher net ratio.

items.title: Example 2: KRW 50M (monthly ~4.17M)
items.body: Insurances ~380K plus rising income tax. Deduction rate ~13-15%. Take-home around 3.5M. More dependents lower the tax.

items.title: Example 3: KRW 100M (monthly 8.33M)
items.body: Pension hits its cap (~278K/month), income tax rises sharply, deduction rate ~18-22%. Higher salaries carry a larger tax share.

faq.question: Why isn't salary ÷ 12 the take-home?
faq.answer: The 4 insurances (~9%) and income/local tax are deducted from gross. KRW 50M is 4.17M monthly gross, but ~3.5M lands in your account after deductions.

faq.question: What is the non-taxable allowance and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Meal allowance (200K/month) and similar are excluded from tax and insurance. A larger non-taxable portion lowers the taxable base and raises take-home. Same salary, different net depending on structure.

faq.question: How much do dependents save?
faq.answer: 150K personal deduction per dependent plus child credits (1 child 150K, 2 children 350K). A family of 4 with 2 children saves tens of thousands of won per year, raising monthly net.

faq.question: Why does this differ from my actual pay?
faq.answer: Real withholding follows the NTS simplified table; this estimates via progressive rates. ±5% variance is normal, reconciled each February at year-end settlement.

faq.question: Are bonuses included?
faq.answer: This assumes even monthly distribution. Bonuses are withheld separately at payment with possibly different rates. Enter base salary for an accurate average monthly net.

faq.question: Is there a pension cap?
faq.answer: Yes. The income base cap (KRW 6.17M as of Jul 2024) fixes pension at ~278K/month for earners above it. Health insurance has a much higher cap.

cautions: 2026 insurance rates + estimated income tax. Actual withholding ±5% via the NTS table.
Assumes 200K non-taxable meal allowance; company structures vary.
Bonuses are withheld separately and differ from this estimate.
Exact tax is reconciled at year-end settlement.
Insurance rates change annually.

visa-days.overview: The Korea stay days calculator computes days stayed, expiry date, and days remaining from your entry date and allowed stay. It tracks 90-day visa-free entry and 30/60/180-day visas, warning you before you overstay. It follows the immigration practice of counting the entry day as day 1 to give an accurate expiry date.
useCases: Track the 90-day visa-free expiry
Check days remaining when planning departure
Prevent illegal overstay in advance
Identify when to apply for an extension
Review cumulative stay after re-entry

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter your entry date (year/month/day) — the passport entry stamp date.
Enter the allowed days or pick a preset (30/60/90/180). Visa-free is usually 90 days.
See days stayed, expiry date, and days remaining. A red warning appears if you've overstayed.

formula.title: Calculation method
formula.body:
Days stayed = (today − entry) + 1 (entry day included)
Expiry = entry + (allowed days − 1)
  e.g. enter Jun 1, 90 days → Jun 1 is day 1, Aug 29 is day 90 (expiry)
Days remaining = expiry − today

Note: counting the entry day as day 1 is Korean immigration practice.
  Status changes may use a different basis.

items.title: Example 1: Enter Jun 1, 90-day visa-free
items.body: Expiry Aug 29 (Jun 1 is day 1). On Jun 30 you've stayed 30 days, 60 remaining. Departing by Aug 29 is fine.

items.title: Example 2: Departing on the expiry day
items.body: Departing on Aug 29 (day 90) is valid. From Aug 30 it's an overstay. Departure must be within the expiry date.

items.title: Example 3: Overstay detection
items.body: Sep 1 is 3 days past expiry (Aug 29). A red warning shows. Overstay leads to fines and entry bans — depart and report immediately.

faq.question: Is the entry day counted as day 1?
faq.answer: Yes. In Korean immigration practice the entry day is day 1. Enter Jun 1 and that's day 1; 90-day visa-free expires on Aug 29 (day 90). This differs from interpretations that count the entry day as day 0.

faq.question: How are the 90 days counted — is departure day included?
faq.answer: Departing by the expiry day (day 90) is valid. If expiry is Aug 29, departing Aug 29 is OK; Aug 30 is a 1-day overstay. Departing the day before expiry is the safe choice.

faq.question: Can I re-enter immediately after 90 days?
faq.answer: Re-entry itself is possible, but repeated short in-and-out trips (border runs) can lead to entry denial. For long stays, get the proper visa (D/E/F).

faq.question: What happens if I overstay?
faq.answer: Fines apply (by days/duration), and you may face entry bans or deportation. Voluntary reporting can reduce penalties — contact immigration before overstaying.

faq.question: When do I apply for an extension?
faq.answer: Usually from 4 months before expiry, online via HiKorea or at an immigration office before expiry. Last-minute applications risk rejection or delay.

faq.question: Can I use this as official proof?
faq.answer: For reference only. Your ARC, HiKorea stay information, and the immigration office are the official sources for exact expiry and status. Use this tool for self-checks.

cautions: Entry day counted as day 1 (immigration practice).
Basis may differ for visa types or status changes.
Border runs (repeated short trips) can cause entry denial.
Verify exact expiry via HiKorea, ARC, or immigration office.
Overstay leads to fines and entry bans.

freelancer-tax.overview: The Korean freelancer 3.3% calculator computes the withholding on freelance/personal-service business income — 3.3% (3% income + 0.3% local). It converts gross to net and net to gross, with annual figures. It's a prepayment reconciled via the May income tax filing, where deducting expenses can yield a refund.
useCases: Check net pay on freelance fees
Negotiate gross vs net amounts in contracts
Back-calculate the gross to invoice for a target net
See annualized income and total withholding
Review withholding totals before the May filing

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter the amount (KRW).
Choose the basis. 'Gross' computes net; 'Net' back-calculates gross.
See the 3% income, 0.3% local, 3.3% total, net pay, and annualized figures.

formula.title: Withholding formula
formula.body:
Income tax = amount × 3%
Local tax = amount × 0.3%
Total withholding = amount × 3.3%
Net = amount − withholding

Reverse (net → gross):
  gross = net / (1 − 0.033)
  e.g. net 967,000 → gross 1,000,000

items.title: Example 1: Gross 1M → net 967,000
items.body: Income tax 30,000 + local 3,000 = withholding 33,000. Net 967,000 KRW.

items.title: Example 2: Target net 3M → back-calculate gross
items.body: Gross = 3M / 0.967 ≈ 3,102,000 KRW. Invoice this to receive 3M net.

items.title: Example 3: 2M/month freelancer annualized
items.body: 2M gross × 12 = 24M annual. ~792K annual withholding. Reconciled in May with expenses/deductions (refund possible).

faq.question: Is 3.3% the final tax?
faq.answer: No. Withholding is a prepayment. The May income tax filing computes the real tax — refund if you have many expenses/deductions, or extra payment if income is high. 3.3% is paid in advance.

faq.question: Why different from an employee's insurances?
faq.answer: Freelancers are business-income earners, not employees, so there's no insurance withholding — only the 3.3% business income withholding. Health and pension are paid separately as a regional subscriber.

faq.question: How much refund from deducting expenses?
faq.answer: Declaring business expenses (equipment, transport, communication) lowers the tax base. Via simplified/standard expense rates or bookkeeping; with low income and high expenses, most of the withholding can be refunded.

faq.question: Must I file income tax?
faq.answer: Yes. Withholding alone isn't the end. Not filing in May incurs a penalty and forfeits any refund. Even with low income, filing is worthwhile for the refund.

faq.question: Multiple payers withheld 3.3% — do I combine them?
faq.answer: Yes. Combine all business income from every payer and file in May. Use the withholding receipts (or Hometax payment statements) to total it.

faq.question: 3.3% vs 8.8% (other income)?
faq.answer: Continuous/repeated services are business income at 3.3%; one-off lectures/manuscripts are other income at 8.8% (after 60% deemed expenses). This tool uses the 3.3% business-income basis.

cautions: 3.3% is a prepayment, not the final tax — reconciled in the May filing.
Refund or extra payment depends on expenses and income size.
Health and pension are paid separately as a regional subscriber.
One-off income (lectures, manuscripts) is other income at 8.8%.
Combine income from multiple payers when filing.

electric-bill.overview: The Korean electricity bill calculator estimates your residential bill from monthly kWh usage. It applies KEPCO's 3-tier progressive tariff (low/high voltage), summer relaxation, climate-environment charge, fuel adjustment, 10% VAT, and 3.7% power fund — giving a figure close to the actual bill. See in advance how much the bill jumps when summer air conditioning pushes you into a higher tier.
useCases: Estimate the bill before heavy summer AC use
See when crossing a tier boundary (200/400 kWh) spikes the cost
Compare low-voltage (house/villa) vs high-voltage (large apt) rates
Simulate savings from dropping one tier through conservation
Verify the electricity portion of apartment maintenance fees

howTo.title: How to use (3 steps)
steps: Enter monthly usage (kWh) from your bill or the KEPCO app.
Choose supply type. Houses/villas are usually low voltage; large apartment complexes use high voltage (bulk contract).
Check summer relaxation for July-August. Tier 1 expands to 300 kWh and tier 2 to 450 kWh.

formula.title: Bill formula (KEPCO residential 2024)
formula.body:
Energy charge = usage per tier × tier rate (progressive)
  Low-voltage tier 1 (0-200kWh) 120.0, tier 2 (-400) 214.6, tier 3 307.3 KRW
  Base fee = base of the highest tier reached (910 / 1,600 / 7,300 KRW)

Climate-environment charge = usage × 9.0 KRW
Fuel adjustment = usage × 5.0 KRW (varies quarterly)
Subtotal = base + energy + climate + fuel
VAT = subtotal × 10%
Power fund = subtotal × 3.7%
Total = sum (rounded down to 10 KRW)

Summer (Jul 1-Aug 31): tier 1 = 300 kWh, tier 2 = 450 kWh

items.title: Example 1: Low voltage 200 kWh (tier 1)
items.body: Base 910 + energy 24,000 (200×120) + climate 1,800 + fuel 1,000 = 27,710. VAT 2,771 + fund 1,020 = total ~31,500 KRW. ~158 KRW/kWh average.

items.title: Example 2: Low voltage 350 kWh (into tier 2)
items.body: Base 1,600 + energy 56,190 (200×120 + 150×214.6) + climate + fuel. Tier 2 rate is 1.8× tier 1, so the extra 150 kWh raises the bill sharply.

items.title: Example 3: Summer 300 kWh savings
items.body: Normally 300 kWh hits tier 2, but summer relaxation keeps it in tier 1. Base 1,600→910 plus 100 kWh at 120 instead of 214.6 saves about KRW 10,000.

faq.question: What exactly is the progressive tariff?
faq.answer: A system charging higher rates as usage rises. Residential has 3 tiers: 120 KRW up to 200 kWh, 214.6 up to 400, 307.3 above. It encourages conservation. Industrial and general rates have no progressive structure.

faq.question: Is my home low or high voltage?
faq.answer: Houses, villas, and multi-family units are mostly low voltage (per-unit metering). Large apartment complexes use a high-voltage bulk contract distributed by management. High-voltage rates are ~12% cheaper. It's shown on the bill.

faq.question: Why are summer/winter bills higher?
faq.answer: AC and heating raise usage, pushing you into higher tiers. The same 100 kWh costs 2.5× more in tier 3 (307) than tier 1 (120). Summer (Jul-Aug) and parts of winter get relaxed tiers, but bills still run higher than mild months.

faq.question: What are the climate and fuel charges?
faq.answer: The climate-environment charge (9 KRW/kWh) covers renewables and emissions; the fuel adjustment (±5) reflects quarterly generation fuel costs (coal, LNG). Both scale with usage.

faq.question: Is there VAT on electricity?
faq.answer: Yes. 10% VAT and a 3.7% power fund are added on top of the subtotal, making the final bill about 14% higher than the electricity subtotal.

faq.question: Are welfare or essential-usage discounts included?
faq.answer: No. The essential-usage guarantee (up to 4,000 KRW off under 200 kWh), multi-child/large-family discounts, and welfare discounts are separate. Check your KEPCO bill for the exact amount.

cautions: KEPCO residential 2024 rates. Tariffs change quarterly/yearly.
Fuel adjustment (±5 KRW) changes quarterly — actual may differ.
Essential-usage, welfare, and multi-child discounts not included.
High-voltage apartment bills depend on the complex's distribution method.
The definitive bill is from KEPCO (paper or Cyber Branch).

name-romanize.overview: The Korean name romanizer converts Hangul names to roman letters using the official Revised Romanization of Korean (2000). Names are romanized syllable-by-syllable without reflecting sound changes (Section 3, Article 4), and surnames also show the conventional passport spellings (Kim, Lee, Park). Get the standard spelling instantly for passports, business cards, papers, and overseas documents.
useCases: Confirm the English spelling for a passport
Write your romanized name on business cards and email signatures
Author name on papers and overseas submissions
Tell a foreign friend how to write your name
English name on remittances and contracts

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Enter a Korean name (e.g., 홍길동). It converts instantly.
Surname and given name split automatically; compound surnames (남궁, 황보) are detected.
You get three forms: official, conventional surname spelling, and hyphenated. Passports usually recommend the conventional surname spelling.

formula.title: Romanization principles (NIKL)
formula.body:
Decompose each syllable into initial/medial/final, then romanize:
  Initials ㄱg ㄴn ㄷd ㄹr ㅁm ㅂb ㅅs ㅇ(silent) ㅈj ㅊch ㅋk ㅌt ㅍp ㅎh ...
  Medials ㅏa ㅓeo ㅗo ㅜu ㅡeu ㅣi ㅐae ㅔe ...
  Finals ㄱk ㄴn ㄹl ㅁm ㅂp ㅇng ...

Name rule: do not reflect sound changes (Sec 3, Art 4)
  홍길동 = Hong + Gil + dong → Hong Gildong

Conventional surname spelling: the most common passport form
  Kim, Lee, Park, Choi, Jung ...

items.title: Example 1: 홍길동 → Hong Gildong
items.body: 홍=Hong, 길동=Gildong. The official spelling equals the conventional one here. Syllable-by-syllable, no sound change.

items.title: Example 2: 김민수 → official Gim Minsu / conventional Kim Minsu
items.body: Officially ㄱ becomes g (Gim), but passports overwhelmingly use Kim. The tool shows both.

items.title: Example 3: Compound surname 남궁민 → Namgung Min
items.body: 남궁 is auto-detected as a two-syllable surname. Surname Namgung, given name Min. Compound surnames are written together.

faq.question: Official or conventional spelling for a passport?
faq.answer: Once set on a passport, it's hard to change, so decide carefully. Match your family's existing spelling (Kim, Lee) if any; for a new one, the conventional spelling is safe. The ministry recommends the standard but allows conventional spellings.

faq.question: Do I hyphenate between given-name syllables?
faq.answer: Passports allow either joined (Gildong) or hyphenated (Gil-dong). Be consistent once chosen. Joined spelling avoids being misread as two separate words abroad.

faq.question: Why don't names reflect sound changes?
faq.answer: The rule deliberately ignores phonetic changes in names to prevent confusion and identity mismatch. Each syllable is spelled consistently letter-by-letter.

faq.question: How were the conventional surname spellings established?
faq.answer: They're the majority spellings entrenched through decades of passport and overseas use. 90%+ of Kims use 'Kim'; 'Lee' dominates for the I surname. They take precedence over the official forms (Gim, I).

faq.question: Are compound surnames recognized?
faq.answer: Yes. Major compound surnames (남궁, 황보, 제갈, 선우, 독고, 사공, 서문, 동방) are auto-detected as two-syllable surnames. Others use the first syllable as the surname.

faq.question: Same spelling for the alien registration card or visa?
faq.answer: This tool is for romanizing Korean nationals' names. Foreigners use the English name on their home-country passport; transliterating into Hangul is a separate process.

cautions: Based on the Revised Romanization of Korean (2000); names don't reflect sound changes.
Passport spelling is hard to change once set — align with family spellings.
Conventional surname spellings (Kim, Lee) differ from the standard but are widely accepted.
Special pronunciations may follow the person's preference.

size-convert.overview: The Korean size converter maps Korean clothing and shoe sizes to US, EU, UK, and Japanese sizes at a glance. Shoes use mm (foot length); clothing uses chest-cm-based Korean labels (men 90/95/100, women 44/55/66) compared to international sizes. Reduce sizing mistakes when shopping K-fashion overseas or on Korean shopping sites.
useCases: Find your size for K-fashion overseas shopping
Foreigners selecting sizes on Korean shopping sites
Convert sizes for gift clothing and shoes
Buy clothes or shoes while traveling in Korea
Cross-check US/EU sizes before ordering online

howTo.title: How to use
steps: Select a category (men's/women's shoes, men's/women's clothing).
Find your Korean size row in the table to see US, EU, UK, and JP sizes.
Note that shoes use mm (foot length) and clothing uses chest cm. Expect ±1 variation by brand.

formula.title: Size basis
formula.body:
Shoes (mondopoint): Korea and Japan use foot length in mm.
  260 = 26.0 cm. US ≈ (mm−180)/5; EU ≈ mm × 1.5 + 2 (approx).
  Men's 260 ≈ US 8 / EU 41 / UK 7.5
  Women's 240 ≈ US 7 / EU 37.5 / UK 4.5

Clothing (chest cm based):
  Men's 90(S)=90cm chest, 95(M)=95cm, 100(L)=100cm
  Women's 44(XS), 55(S), 66(M), 77(L), 88(XL) traditional labels
  → mapped to US/EU standard labels

items.title: Example 1: Men's shoe 270 → US 9
items.body: Korean 270mm = 27.0cm = US 9 / EU 42.5 / UK 8.5 / JP 27.0. Pick US 9 when buying Nike or Adidas.

items.title: Example 2: Women's clothing 55 → US 4
items.body: Korean women's 55 = S = 85cm chest ≈ US 4 / EU 36 / UK 8. Choose S or US 4 on Zara or H&M.

items.title: Example 3: Men's clothing 100 → L
items.body: Korean men's 100 = 100cm chest = US L / EU 50 / UK 40. American brand L or EU 50.

faq.question: Why are Korean shoe sizes numbers like 250, 260?
faq.answer: They are foot length in mm (mondopoint). 250 = 25.0 cm foot length. Japan uses the same (in cm). It's the most intuitive measure of the foot, used across Korea and Japan.

faq.question: What do women's 44/55/66 mean?
faq.answer: Traditional labels from 1980s standard body measurements. 55 is the standard (S), 44 is smaller (XS), and 66/77/88 are larger. The numbers themselves don't carry literal meaning — they're entrenched labels.

faq.question: Brands differ — is this chart accurate?
faq.answer: This chart uses KS standards + international references as general values. Actual fit varies ±1 size by brand and cut (slim/oversized). Cross-check the brand's cm measurements when possible.

faq.question: Are US shoe sizes different for men and women?
faq.answer: Yes. For the same foot length, US labels differ by gender (women ~1.5 larger). This chart separates men and women, so use the right category. EU is unisex.

faq.question: Are children's sizes included?
faq.answer: No, this chart is for adults. Children's sizing uses a separate system (foot mm is the same, but clothing is height/age-based).

faq.question: Any tips for sizing up/down when importing?
faq.answer: Shoes are usually true to size, but US brands sometimes run large — size down 0.5 for narrow feet. Clothing from US/EU runs larger than Asian fit, so consider sizing down. Reviews and cm measurements are safest.

cautions: General values from KS standards + international charts. ±1 size variation by brand/fit.
Shoes use mm (foot length); clothing uses chest cm.
US shoe labels differ by gender — pick the right category.
For exact fit, cross-check the brand's cm measurements.
Children's sizes not included (adults only).

capital-gains-tax.overview: The Korean real estate capital gains tax calculator estimates tax from sale price, purchase price, and holding period. From the capital gain it subtracts the long-term holding deduction (up to 30% general, up to 80% for a single-house household) and the KRW 2.5M basic deduction, applies the 8-bracket progressive rate, adds 10% local tax, and shows total tax and after-tax gain. Short-term penalty rates (under 2 years) are included.
useCases: Simulate tax before selling property
Compare long-term deduction by holding period
Check savings from the single-house benefit
See short-term (under 2 years) penalty rates
Estimate after-tax net proceeds

howTo.title: How to use (5 steps)
steps: Enter sale price and purchase price.
Enter expenses (acquisition tax, brokerage, capital improvements). They reduce the gain.
Enter holding period (years). The long-term deduction applies from 3 years.
Choose asset type (house/land) and single-house status. For a single house, residing period also affects the deduction.
Results show gain, long-term deduction, tax base, calculated tax, local tax, and after-tax gain.

formula.title: Capital gains tax formula (Income Tax Act)
formula.body:
Gain = sale price − purchase price − expenses
Long-term deduction = gain × rate
  General: 6% at 3 years, +2%/year, capped 30% at 15 years
  Single house: 4%/year holding + 4%/year residing, max 80%
Taxable income = gain − long-term deduction
Tax base = taxable income − KRW 2.5M basic deduction
Calculated tax = base × progressive rate − deduction (same 8 brackets as income tax)
Local income tax = calculated tax × 10%

Short-term (penalty): house under 1yr 70%, under 2yr 60%
  Land/building under 1yr 50%, under 2yr 40%

items.title: Example 1: Sale 500M, buy 300M, 5 years (general house)
items.body: Gain 180M (after 20M expenses). Long-term 10% (6%+2×2%) = 18M. Base 155M. 35% bracket → ~39M calculated + 3.9M local = ~42.9M KRW.

items.title: Example 2: Single house, 10 years held & resided
items.body: Even a 1B gain gets 80% deduction = 800M off. Tax base drops to ~190M. Note: if sale is under KRW 1.2B it's fully exempt (this tool covers the taxable case).

items.title: Example 3: Short-term, under 1 year
items.body: Gain 100M, held 6 months. No long-term deduction (under 3 years). Base 97.5M × 70% = 68.25M + local. Short-term flips carry very high tax.

faq.question: Is a single house always tax-exempt?
faq.answer: Exempt if sale price is under KRW 1.2B and held 2+ years (2 years residing in regulated areas). The portion above 1.2B is taxed, where the up-to-80% long-term deduction gives big relief. This calculator covers the taxable case.

faq.question: Why is the long-term deduction so large?
faq.answer: It accounts for inflation and policy support for long holdings. General is up to 30%, but a single house (holding + residing, up to 40% each) reaches 80%, sharply lowering tax for 10+ year owner-occupants.

faq.question: Why are short-term rates so high?
faq.answer: To curb speculative flipping. A house held under 1 year is taxed at 70% — higher than the top progressive bracket (45%). Hold 2+ years for the normal progressive rate.

faq.question: What counts as deductible expenses?
